---
title: "Population & Growth"
description: "Population over time, growth rate, components of change, non-permanent residents, age structure & aging, and migration within Canada"
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
import re
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pipeline.config import CANADA_COLOR, get_data_date
from pipeline.charts import (single_line, single_bar, peer_comparison_line,
                             ranked_bar, population_pyramid)
```

## Canada's population over time

Canada's population has grown steadily since the mid-20th century. This chart shows quarterly population estimates for Canada as a whole.

```{python}
#| echo: false
df = pd.read_csv("data/population/statcan_population_quarterly.csv", parse_dates=["date"])

# National total — Canada. Plot in millions for readability; open on the full record.
canada = df[df["geography"] == "Canada"].copy()
canada["pop_m"] = canada["population"] / 1e6

fig = single_line(canada, "date", "pop_m", title="", yaxis_title="Population (millions)",
                  rangeslider=True, initial_full=True, yaxis_tickformat=".0f",
                  customdata=canada["date"].dt.quarter,
                  hovertemplate="<b>%{x|%Y} Q%{customdata}</b><br>Population: %{y:.2f} million<extra></extra>",
                  source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 17-10-0009-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date('data/population/statcan_population_quarterly.csv')}")
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## Population by province and territory

Select provinces and territories to compare using the legend. Click a name to toggle it; double-click to isolate one. The three territories appear as low, near-flat lines: together they are home to under 0.13 million people, against more than 15 million in Ontario.

```{python}
#| echo: false
# Provincial + territorial breakdown (all 13; shown in millions)
provinces = df[df["geography"] != "Canada"].copy()
provinces["pop_m"] = provinces["population"] / 1e6

# Order by latest population (largest first); include every province AND territory
latest = provinces.groupby("geography")["population"].last().sort_values(ascending=False)
top_provinces = latest.index.tolist()

# Color palette
colors = [
    "#1f77b4", "#ff7f0e", "#2ca02c", "#d62728", "#9467bd",
    "#8c564b", "#e377c2", "#7f7f7f", "#bcbd22", "#17becf",
    "#aec7e8", "#ffbb78", "#98df8a", "#ff9896", "#c5b0d5",
]

fig = go.Figure()
for i, geo in enumerate(top_provinces):
    subset = provinces[provinces["geography"] == geo]
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=subset["date"],
        y=subset["pop_m"],
        name=geo,
        mode="lines",
        line=dict(color=colors[i % len(colors)], width=2),
        hovertemplate=f"<b>{geo}</b><br>%{{x|%Y}} Q%{{customdata}}: %{{y:.2f}} million<extra></extra>",
        customdata=subset["date"].dt.quarter,
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="",
    yaxis_title="Population (millions)",
    plot_bgcolor="white",
    xaxis=dict(
        gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
        rangeselector=dict(
            buttons=[
                dict(count=1, label="1Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=2, label="2Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=5, label="5Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=10, label="10Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=20, label="20Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=50, label="50Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(step="all", label="All"),
            ],
            x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.01,
        ),
        rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.08, bgcolor="#f5f5f5"),
        range=["1999-01-01", provinces["date"].max()],
    ),
    yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
    hovermode="x unified",
    legend=dict(
        orientation="v", yanchor="top", y=1,
        xanchor="left", x=1.02,
        itemclick="toggle", itemdoubleclick="toggleothers",
    ),
    margin=dict(b=110, t=40, r=160),
    annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 17-10-0009-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date('data/population/statcan_population_quarterly.csv')}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.30,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"),
    )],
)
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## Population growth rate

Year-over-year percentage change in Canada's quarterly population. This is derived from the same quarterly estimates shown above — each quarter is compared to the same quarter one year earlier.

```{python}
#| echo: false
# Compute YoY growth rate for Canada
canada_growth = df[df["geography"] == "Canada"].copy()
canada_growth = canada_growth.sort_values("date")
canada_growth["pop_yoy_pct"] = canada_growth["population"].pct_change(periods=4) * 100
canada_growth = canada_growth.dropna(subset=["pop_yoy_pct"])

fig = single_bar(canada_growth, "date", "pop_yoy_pct", title="",
                 yaxis_title="Year-over-Year Growth (%)", rangeslider=True,
                 customdata=canada_growth["date"].dt.quarter,
                 hovertemplate="<b>%{x|%Y} Q%{customdata}</b><br>Growth: %{y:.2f}%<extra></extra>",
                 source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 17-10-0009-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date('data/population/statcan_population_quarterly.csv')}")
fig.update_yaxes(range=[0, 5])
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## Where Canada's population growth comes from

Population growth breaks into three parts: **natural increase** (births minus deaths), **net permanent immigration** (new permanent residents minus people leaving), and **net temporary residents** (the non-permanent population — people on work and study permits, plus asylum claimants). Stacking them shows what actually drove growth each year; the surge of the early 2020s was overwhelmingly temporary residents, and that component has since turned negative. **Use the dropdown to isolate a single component** or view all three stacked. For a sense of scale, a net change of about 170,000 people in a year is roughly a city the size of Guelph, Kingston, or Greater Sudbury.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
components_path = "data/population/statcan_population_components.csv"
if os.path.exists(components_path):
    comp = pd.read_csv(components_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    comp = comp[comp["geography"] == "Canada"]
    piv = comp.pivot_table(index="date", columns="component", values="value", aggfunc="sum").sort_index()
    natural = piv["Births"] - piv["Deaths"]
    permanent = piv["Immigrants"] - piv["Net emigration"]
    temporary = piv["Net non-permanent residents"]
    yrs = piv.index.year
    # Plot in thousands of persons for readability.
    cats = [("Natural increase (births − deaths)", natural / 1e3, "#59a14f"),
            ("Net permanent immigration", permanent / 1e3, "#4e79a7"),
            ("Net temporary residents", temporary / 1e3, "#f28e2b")]
    figp = go.Figure()
    for name, series, col in cats:
        figp.add_trace(go.Bar(x=yrs, y=series.values, name=name, marker_color=col,
            hovertemplate=name + ": %{y:,.0f}k<extra></extra>"))
    figp.add_hline(y=0, line_color="#888", line_width=1)
    # Dropdown: all three stacked (default) or one component on its own.
    n = len(cats)
    sel = [dict(label="All components (stacked)", method="update",
                args=[{"visible": [True] * n}])]
    for k, (nm, _, _) in enumerate(cats):
        sel.append(dict(label=nm, method="update",
                        args=[{"visible": [j == k for j in range(n)]}]))
    figp.update_layout(barmode="relative", plot_bgcolor="white", height=480, bargap=0.15,
        yaxis=dict(title="Net change per year (thousands of persons)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
                   tickformat=",", zeroline=False),
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0"), hovermode="x unified",
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="top", y=-0.12, xanchor="left", x=0),
        margin=dict(t=70, b=120, r=20),
        updatemenus=[dict(buttons=sel, active=0, x=1, xanchor="right", y=1.14,
                          yanchor="top", bgcolor="white", bordercolor="#ccc",
                          borderwidth=1, showactive=True)])
    figp.add_annotation(text="Show:", xref="paper", yref="paper", x=1, xanchor="right",
                        y=1.18, yanchor="bottom", showarrow=False, font=dict(size=11, color="#666"))
    figp.add_annotation(text=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 17-10-0008-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(components_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.30, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    figp.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Population components data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Non-permanent residents

The number of non-permanent residents — people in Canada on work or study permits, or as asylum claimants and protected persons — has risen sharply in recent years. It is central to current debates about the pace of population growth, housing demand, and the capacity of public services. The line shows the total across all permit types.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
npr_path = "data/population/statcan_temp_residents.csv"
if os.path.exists(npr_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(npr_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    df["npr_m"] = df["npr_count"] / 1e6
    fig = single_line(df, "date", "npr_m", title="",
                      yaxis_title="Non-permanent residents (millions)", rangeslider=True,
                      yaxis_tickformat=".1f",
                      hovertemplate="<b>%{x|%Y}</b><br>%{y:.2f} million non-permanent residents<extra></extra>",
                      source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 17-10-0121-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(npr_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Non-permanent residents data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Age structure

How old is Canada? The pyramid below shows the full age distribution — every single
year of age, men to the left, women to the right. **Drag the slider** to watch half a
century of change: the wide base of the early 1970s (the tail of the baby boom)
narrows as births decline, and the boomer bulge climbs the pyramid into retirement.
Recent years add a new feature — a visible widening through the 20s and 30s as
immigration and non-permanent residents, who arrive young, fill in the working ages.
The white line traces the **net difference between women and men** at each age.

```{python}
#| echo: false
ages_path = "data/population/statcan_age_structure.csv"
if os.path.exists(ages_path):
    agedf = pd.read_csv(ages_path)
    sy = agedf[(agedf["uom"] == "Persons") & agedf["gender"].isin(["Men", "Women"])].copy()

    def _age_num(s):
        s = str(s).strip()
        if s == "90 years and older":      # consistent top bucket across all years
            return 90
        m = re.fullmatch(r"(\d+) years?", s)
        a = int(m.group(1)) if m else None
        return a if a is not None and a <= 89 else None

    sy["age"] = sy["age_group"].map(_age_num)
    sy = sy.dropna(subset=["age"])
    fig = population_pyramid(sy,
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 17-10-0005-01; ages 90+ grouped | Data as of: {get_data_date(ages_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Age-structure data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

### Median age

Half of Canadians are older than this line. The median age rose from about 26 in 1971
to about 41 today — driven by longer lives and smaller families. The slight dip in
recent years is due to immigration.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(ages_path):
    med = agedf[(agedf["uom"] == "Years") & (agedf["age_group"] == "Median age")
                & (agedf["gender"] == "Total")].copy()
    med["date"] = pd.to_datetime(med["year"].astype(int).astype(str), format="%Y")
    fig = single_line(med, "date", "value", title="", yaxis_title="Median age (years)",
        rangeslider=True, initial_full=True,
        hovertemplate="<b>%{x|%Y}</b><br>Median age: %{y:.1f} years<extra></extra>",
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 17-10-0005-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(ages_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

### Aging among peer countries

The old-age dependency ratio — people 65 and over per 100 people of working age
(15–64) — is the standard gauge of how the population's weight is shifting onto
fewer working-age shoulders. Every peer country is aging; Canada sits in the middle
of the pack, well below Japan and Italy.

```{python}
#| echo: false
dep_path = "data/population/worldbank_old_age_dependency.csv"
if os.path.exists(dep_path):
    dep = pd.read_csv(dep_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(dep, "year", "old_age_dependency", "",
        "People 65+ per 100 aged 15–64", show_average=True, topical=["JPN", "ITA"],
        source_note=f"Source: World Bank WDI (UN World Population Prospects) | Data as of: {get_data_date(dep_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Old-age dependency data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(dep_path):
    fig = ranked_bar(dep, "old_age_dependency", "People 65+ per 100 aged 15–64",
        f"Source: World Bank WDI (UN World Population Prospects) | Data as of: {get_data_date(dep_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.1f}<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

### Fertility

The total fertility rate is the average number of children a woman would have over
her lifetime at current age-specific rates. A rate of about 2.1 keeps a population
stable without immigration — the dashed line below. Canada fell below that level in
the early 1970s and has set new record lows in recent years; immigration is now the
engine of population growth. Most peer countries are on the same path.

```{python}
#| echo: false
fert_path = "data/population/worldbank_fertility_rate.csv"
if os.path.exists(fert_path):
    fert = pd.read_csv(fert_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(fert, "year", "fertility_rate", "",
        "Births per woman", show_average=True, initial_visible=["USA"],
        source_note=f"Source: World Bank WDI (UN World Population Prospects) | Data as of: {get_data_date(fert_path)}")
    fig.add_hline(y=2.1, line_dash="dash", line_color="#555", line_width=1,
                  annotation_text="Replacement level (≈2.1)",
                  annotation_position="top right",
                  annotation_font=dict(size=10, color="#555"))
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Fertility data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(fert_path):
    fig = ranked_bar(fert, "fertility_rate", "Births per woman",
        f"Source: World Bank WDI (UN World Population Prospects) | Data as of: {get_data_date(fert_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.2f}<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

### Median age by neighbourhood

Where do the young and the old actually live? A census-tract map of **median age**
— with each neighbourhood's full age breakdown in the hover — is on its own page
(it carries ~3 MB of boundary data):

**[Median age by neighbourhood →](age-neighbourhoods.qmd)**

It pairs naturally with the [median household income](../income/neighbourhoods.qmd)
and [home value](../housing/neighbourhoods.qmd) maps — the same census tracts, so
you can flip between who lives somewhere and what living there costs.

## Moving within Canada

Beyond immigration, Canadians relocate between provinces — and the direction of
that flow says a lot about relative job markets and housing costs. The bars show
**net** interprovincial migration (movers in minus movers out) by July–June year,
one province at a time — **use the dropdown to switch province**. Alberta's recent
net inflows are the largest in the 60-year record, ahead of its 1981 and 2006
energy-boom peaks, while Ontario's recent net outflows are also the largest on
record.

```{python}
#| echo: false
mig_path = "data/population/statcan_interprovincial_migration.csv"
if os.path.exists(mig_path):
    mig = pd.read_csv(mig_path)
    fig = single_bar(mig, "year", "net_migration", title="",
        yaxis_title="Net interprovincial migration (persons)",
        select_col="geography", default_option="Alberta", height=480,
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 17-10-0020-01; July–June years labelled by ending year | Data as of: {get_data_date(mig_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Interprovincial migration data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Download data

- [Population Quarterly Estimates (CSV)](../data/population/statcan_population_quarterly.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 17-10-0009-01
- [Non-Permanent Residents (CSV)](../data/population/statcan_temp_residents.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 17-10-0121-01
- [Population by Age & Gender, 1971– (CSV)](../data/population/statcan_age_structure.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 17-10-0005-01
- [Net Interprovincial Migration (CSV)](../data/population/statcan_interprovincial_migration.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 17-10-0020-01
- [Total Fertility Rate, 17 peers (CSV)](../data/population/worldbank_fertility_rate.csv) — World Bank WDI (UN World Population Prospects)
- [Old-Age Dependency Ratio, 17 peers (CSV)](../data/population/worldbank_old_age_dependency.csv) — World Bank WDI (UN World Population Prospects)
- [Median Age & Age Mix by Census Tract (CSV)](../data/geo/statcan_ct_age_2021.csv) — 2021 Census of Population
